shower.thought: We spend our whole lives running from boredom, but boredom might be the only time we're actually honest with ourselves.
The people you miss are missing you back, but in different moments, so you're never actually sad at the same time.
You'll never know if you have good taste or just consistent taste.
Every conversation you have is with a version of that person that only exists in your head.
Silence is the only thing that gets better the longer you hold it.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you're technically living in a different past than everyone else thinks you lived.
Death is the only thing guaranteed to interrupt everything you're worried about.
The older you get, the more you realize that most people are just doing their best impression of an adult.
You've probably had the same thought as someone on the other side of the world at the exact same second, and you'll never know it.
Confidence is just the absence of imagining what other people are thinking about you.
We treat our phones like they're extensions of our brains, but they remember everything we forget.
The person you were ten years ago would be shocked not by how much you've changed, but by how much you haven't.
You can't actually imagine a new color, which means there's a limit to what you can think, and you hit it every time.
Happiness might just be the moment before you realize you should worry about losing it.
If you could see yourself from the outside, you'd probably be unrecognizable to you.
The more specific your memory of something, the less likely it is to be accurate.
You spend your childhood wanting to be older and your adulthood wanting to be younger, but you're never actually satisfied with now.
Regret only works backwards, which means it's the most useless emotion we have.
People don't really change—they just find better excuses for being the same.
Every time you learn something new, you're not actually smarter; you're just differently confused.

motivational.thought: # 35 Original Motivational Quotes
The person who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the person doing it. — Chinese Proverb
Your only real competition is the version of yourself from yesterday. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing what you want most over what you want now. — Unknown
Most people fail not because they aim too high and miss, but because they aim too low and hit. — Les Brown
The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek. — Joseph Campbell
You can't control the wind, but you can adjust your sails. — Dolly Parton
Progress is progress, no matter how small. A step forward is still forward. — Unknown
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. — Tony Robbins
Regret is the tax you pay for inaction. — Unknown
Your potential is endless, but your time is not. Spend it wisely. — Unknown
The only person who believes in you as much as you do is yourself. Make it count. — Unknown
Pain is temporary. Regret lasts forever. — Unknown
What lies behind us and what lies before us are tiny matters compared to what lies within us. — Ralph Waldo Emerson
Mediocrity is a choice. Excellence is a habit. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are not behind. You are exactly where you need to be. — Unknown
The person who finishes is further ahead than the person who never starts. — Unknown
Your struggles today are building the strength of tomorrow. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of contentment and the poison of potential. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now. — Chinese Proverb

darkjoke.thought:
I told my friend he was living in denial.
He said denial isn't just a river in Egypt—it's also a very affordable housing option.

My dad said, "Son, I'm not mad, just disappointed."
I said, "Thanks for the upgrade from our usual dynamic."

I went to a motivational seminar called "You Are Enough."
Turns out I was enough to pay for it, but not enough to get my money back.

My therapist asked why I keep sabotaging my relationships.
I said, "Because at least I'm consistent at something."

I asked my friend how his new diet was going.
He said, "Great! I've lost three friends and my sense of humor."

My ex texted me, "I miss you."
I texted back, "You miss me or you miss having someone to blame your problems on?"
She blocked me, so I guess the answer was option two.

I told my coworker she had great energy.
She said, "Thanks, I'm powered by spite and student loans."

My mom asked if I was seeing anyone.
I said, "Yeah, a therapist. Twice a week."
She said, "That's not what I meant."
I said, "I know."

I went to a self-help book signing.
The author asked what I was struggling with most.
I pointed to the book I just bought.

My friend said he was taking a "digital detox."
Three days later he was texting me paragraphs about how bored he was.

I asked my dentist why teeth cleaning costs so much.
He said, "You're paying for my student loans and the privilege of hearing about my divorce."

My doctor said I needed to cut back on my drinking.
I asked, "Or what—I'll develop a serious alcohol problem?"
He said, "You already have one. I'm just trying to slow it down."

I told my girlfriend she was overthinking things.
She said, "That's rich coming from someone who's been analyzing that joke you made three weeks ago."
I said, "It wasn't that bad."
She said, "Exactly."

My brother said he was joining a cult.
I said, "Finally, a structured environment for your personality issues."

I asked my accountant if I was doing okay financially.
He just stared at his computer screen and poured a second whiskey at 10 AM.

My friend said, "I'm starting to think nobody likes me."
I said, "Don't be so hard on yourself—I'm sure there are dozens of people who are indifferent to you."

I went to a grief counselor after my breakup.
She said, "How are you feeling?"
I said, "Like I'm paying someone to agree that my ex was terrible."
She said, "That's $150, please come back next week."

My wife asked if I was listening to her.
I said, "Of course," without actually hearing a word.
She said, "What did I just say?"
I said, "Something important, I'm sure."
She's sleeping in the other room, but honestly, the silence is therapeutic.

I told my friend I was struggling with imposter syndrome.
He said, "You're not an imposter. You're exactly as incompetent as you think you are."
It was oddly validating.

My therapist asked about my childhood trauma.
I said, "Do you have all afternoon?"
She said, "No, but I bill hourly, so please continue."
